food_name: Chicken Cabbage Salad (Goi Ga Bap Cai)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers chicken salad as a category; no specific Vietnamese chicken cabbage salad entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest parent for a prepared chicken salad dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Vietnamese chicken cabbage salad specifically. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Vietnamese chicken cabbage salad or sufficiently close prepared salad concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Goi Ga Bap Cai is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a plated salad with shredded chicken, shredded cabbage, herbs, and dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Salad, Vietnamese-style — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (Chicken breast, cooked; Cabbage, raw; Fish sauce; Lime juice; Peanuts, roasted) and FAO/INFOODS SEA nutrient composition tables for Southeast Asian dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Goi Ga Bap Cai exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), raw cabbage (FDC ID 169975), fish sauce (FDC ID 173580), lime juice (FDC ID 167747), and roasted peanuts (FDC ID 172430), combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data for Vietnamese salad dishes. Proportions based on standard recipe composition: approximately 40% cooked chicken, 35% cabbage, 10% herbs and aromatics, 10% dressing (fish sauce, lime, sugar, chilli), 5% peanuts and fried shallots.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed salad with lean protein and non-starchy vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Goi Ga Bap Cai. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed salad dishes with lean poultry and non-starchy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is dominated by cabbage (GI ~10), chicken (negligible GI contribution), and a fish sauce–lime dressing with modest sugar content. The small amount of added sugar and peanuts moderately elevates GI above pure non-starchy vegetable baseline. GL per serving = (45 × 19.5 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 8.8.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the fish sauce–based dressing contributes approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and portion control or requesting reduced dressing is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Goi Ga Bap Cai has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of approximately 8.8 per standard serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the modest added sugar in the dressing should be noted and dressing quantity moderated.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein, low saturated fat, and vegetable-dominant composition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean chicken breast with low saturated fat (approximately 0.9 g per 100 g), a high proportion of non-starchy vegetables, and beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern, though the elevated sodium from fish sauce warrants attention for individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goi Ga Bap Cai (Gỏi Gà Bắp Cải) is a Vietnamese shredded chicken and cabbage salad that is widely popular across Vietnam and is served in Vietnamese restaurants throughout Singapore. The dish is prepared by poaching chicken breast until just cooked, then shredding it finely by hand. Thinly shredded white or green cabbage forms the bulk of the salad, combined with thinly sliced onion, Vietnamese coriander (rau răm), and sometimes mint or perilla. The dressing is a classic Vietnamese sweet-sour-savoury combination of fish sauce, fresh lime juice, sugar, minced garlic, and sliced fresh chilli. The salad is finished with crispy fried shallots and crushed roasted peanuts, which add textural contrast. The flavour profile is bright, tangy, and herbaceous with a savoury umami depth from the fish sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in calories (approximately 112 kcal per 100 g), high in lean protein (approximately 11.8 g per 100 g), low in saturated fat, and rich in vitamin C and B vitamins from the cabbage and herbs. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, as fish sauce contributes significantly to the sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified fish sauce and chicken.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Goi Ga Bap Cai was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the primary ingredients and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data. GI was estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed salad dishes with lean protein and non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the natural ingredient composition of this dish (no hydrogenated fats used). The dish is classified under Singapore cuisine in the input record but is of Vietnamese origin and is served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore; cuisine has been corrected to Vietnamese with region_of_origin noting Singapore context. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce in the dressing; individuals with hypertension or sodium-restricted diets should moderate portion size or request reduced dressing. GI estimated at 45 (low) by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database for mixed salad with lean poultry and non-starchy vegetables; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in approved sources. GL per 300 g serving = (45 × 19.5) / 100 = 8.8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not directly measured for this dish.
